text.fullText: February 13, 2026 Submitted via Regulations.gov Jessica Kramer, Assistant Administrator Office of Water U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: The Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe (Band) is a Federally-recognized Anishinaabe Tribe, located in east-central Minnesota, with our reservation established under the 1855 Treaty of Washington (10 Stat. 1165) with treaty and reserved rights in the 1837 Treaty of St. Peters (7 Stat. 536) and 1842 Treaty of La Pointe (7 Stat. 591) treaty-ceded territories spanning from east central Minnesota, across northern Wisconsin, and into western portion of the upper peninsula of Michigan, including the portions of Lake Superior in Minnesota, Wisconsin, and Michigan. A portion of our Reservation in Mille Lacs County is within the Minneapolis–St. Paul– Bloomington MN–WI Metropolitan Statistical Area (Twin Cities) containing two of our nine statutory communities, while seven of our other statutory communities are located in Aitkin and Pine Counties, along with our Urban Service Area located in the heart of the Twin Cities. With this diverse perspective of having an urban, suburban, and rural mix of our communities, we thank the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for this opportunity to provide our comments on the proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Regulations published at 91 FR 2008 on January 15, 2026. We are submitting these comments in strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed revisions. Our Treaty and Reserved Rights, including rights to hunt, fish, and gather, depend fundamentally on the health and integrity of the waters within our ceded territories and reservation lands, and the robust implementation of Clean Water Act Section 401 is essential to protecting those waters. Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe INTRODUCTION For decades, Clean Water Act Section 401 has served as a critical tool enabling states and Tribes to ensure that federally-permitted projects comply with water quality standards and protect the integrity of waters within their jurisdictions. Section 401 recognizes the primary responsibility and rights of states and Tribes to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution in their waters, while ensuring appropriate coordination with Federal licensing and permitting agencies. The Band has consistently emphasized that Section 401’s protections are essential to maintaining water quality, supporting fish and wildlife populations, preserving manoomin (wild rice, Zizania palustris) habitat, and sustaining the cultural practices that define us as a people. The Band previously participated in consultation and provided extensive input on the 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule. That rule, which took effect in September 2023, established a balanced framework that respects state and Tribal authority while providing clear processes and timelines for certification. The 2023 Rule underwent rigorous public participation, extensive Tribal consultation, and careful consideration of stakeholder input. It provides clear guidance on the scope of certification, transparent procedures, and mechanisms to address implementation challenges while maintaining strong protections for water quality. Despite the thorough development and recent implementation of the 2023 Rule, EPA now proposes sweeping revisions that would fundamentally alter the Section 401 certification process in ways that significantly restrict state and Tribal authority, create uncertainty and inefficiency, and undermine water quality protection. The proposed rule dramatically narrows the scope of certification, imposes rigid procedural requirements, eliminates important flexibility for certifying authorities, and removes established provisions for Tribal treatment as states. These changes are particularly concerning for the Band, as they would limit our ability to protect waters essential to our treaty rights and would shift the burden of water quality protection onto Tribes without providing adequate resources or technical support. DISCUSSION I. The Proposed Standardization of Request for Certification Contents Inappropriately Limits Certifying Authority Discretion and Creates Gaps in Information Necessary for Informed Decision-Making 2 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe The proposed rule would eliminate the authority of certifying authorities to define additional contents for certification requests beyond EPA’s prescribed minimum requirements. Under the current 2023 Rule, certifying authorities may establish additional information requirements tailored to their specific regulatory programs, water quality standards, and local conditions. The proposed rule would replace this flexible approach with a single, federally- mandated list of required components, preventing states and Tribes from requesting information necessary to conduct thorough reviews of project impacts on water quality. This change is problematic for several reasons. First, water quality standards, regulatory programs, and environmental conditions vary significantly across jurisdictions. The information necessary for a meaningful certification review in one state or Tribal territory may differ substantially from what is needed in another. For example, states and Tribes with narrative water quality standards for aquatic life protection may need biological assessment data, habitat evaluations, and food web analyses that go beyond the basic discharge characterization information required by the proposed rule. States and Tribes with antidegradation policies may need comprehensive analyses of existing water quality, available assimilative capacity, and alternatives analysis. Jurisdictions with endangered species concerns may need species-specific impact assessments. The proposed rule’s one-size-fits-all approach fails to account for this diversity and would prevent certifying authorities from obtaining the information necessary to conduct reviews consistent with their water quality requirements. Second, the proposed required components focus primarily on direct discharge characteristics and largely exclude information about project operations, cumulative impacts, secondary impacts, and indirect effects on water quality. While the proposed rule requires applicants to include “readily available water quality-related materials on any potential discharges,” this language is vague and would likely be interpreted narrowly to exclude comprehensive environmental assessments, habitat impact studies, cumulative effects analyses, and other materials that certifying authorities currently use to evaluate whether projects will comply with all applicable water quality requirements. The Band regularly needs such information to assess whether projects affecting our waters will comply with water quality standards for aquatic life, threatened and endangered species, cultural resources including manoomin habitat, and other protected uses. Third, the justification for this change—that state and Tribal authority to define additional requirements could “blur this bright-line” regarding when the statutory review clock begins—is not supported by the case law cited or by implementation experience. The Second Circuit’s decision in New York State Department of Environmental Conservation v. FERC addressed a situation where a certifying authority claimed the review clock did not start until it 3 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe deemed an application “complete,” with completeness determined subjectively and without clear standards. The court held that the review clock starts upon “receipt” of a certification request, but did not hold that EPA must define a single list of required contents or that certifying authorities cannot establish their own requirements in advance. To the contrary, the decision recognizes that certifying authorities may establish clear requirements for what constitutes a “request for certification,” and that the clock starts when those requirements are met. The current 2023 Rule implements this principle by allowing certifying authorities to establish and publish their requirements in advance, providing clear, objective standards for when the review period begins. Moreover, EPA’s concern about certifying authorities requesting “information unrelated to certification of project-related discharges” is addressed through other mechanisms, including the scope of certification provisions, judicial review, and EPA oversight. If a certifying authority imposes requirements that exceed its statutory authority, applicants have recourse through the courts and through EPA’s authority to review state and Tribal certification decisions. Creating rigid federal mandates that prevent all certifying authorities from tailoring information requirements to their specific needs is a disproportionate response that will harm water quality protection without solving the problems EPA identifies. The Band urges EPA to retain the current approach allowing certifying authorities to define additional required contents for certification requests, while providing guidance to ensure that such requirements are clearly stated in advance, objectively determinable, related to water quality, and within the scope of Section 401. This balanced approach respects cooperative federalism, accommodates regional variation, and ensures that certifying authorities have the information needed to make informed decisions while maintaining predictability about when the review period begins. II. The Proposed Elimination of Reasonable Extensions and Prohibition on Withdrawal and Resubmittal Create Unrealistic Timelines That Will Force Premature Decisions and Undermine Water Quality Protection The proposed rule would eliminate the current provisions allowing automatic extensions to the reasonable period of time for public notice procedures and force majeure events, and would explicitly prohibit certifying authorities from requesting that applicants withdraw and resubmit certification requests. These changes would impose rigid, inflexible timelines that fail to account for legitimate circumstances requiring additional time and would pressure certifying authorities to make premature decisions with incomplete information. Under current law and the 2023 Rule, certifying authorities must act “within a reasonable period of time (which shall not exceed one year)” after receipt of a certification request. This 4 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe language recognizes that “reasonable” varies depending on project complexity, information availability, public participation requirements, and other factors. The automatic extension provisions in the current rule acknowledge that certain circumstances—such as requirements for extended public notice periods or unexpected events like natural disasters—may necessitate additional time beyond the initial reasonable period without indicating any failure to act in good faith. The proposed elimination of these extensions is particularly problematic for Tribal governments, which often face unique challenges in conducting Section 401 reviews. Tribal environmental programs are frequently understaffed and under-resourced compared to state programs, making it difficult to complete complex reviews within compressed timeframes. Tribal governments may need to conduct government-to-government consultation with other agencies, coordinate with multiple Tribal departments, engage with Tribal councils and traditional leaders, conduct cultural resource assessments, and ensure adequate opportunities for community input. These processes are essential to fulfilling Tribal governmental responsibilities and ensuring that certification decisions reflect Tribal values and treaty rights, but they may require more time than the rigid deadlines proposed by EPA. Similarly, the proposed prohibition on requesting withdrawal and resubmittal would eliminate an important tool that certifying authorities currently use to address situations where applications are incomplete, new information comes to light, project designs change, or additional analysis is needed. While EPA characterizes this practice as a “delay tactic,” in reality it is often a mechanism for ensuring that certification reviews are based on complete and accurate information. When significant new information becomes available late in the review process, or when an applicant proposes substantial project modifications, requesting withdrawal and resubmittal allows for a comprehensive review of the revised proposal rather than forcing the certifying authority to either approve a project based on outdated information or deny certification based on an incomplete record. The Band has used the withdrawal and resubmittal process in situations where applicants have submitted insufficient information, where project designs have changed substantially during the review period, and where new environmental concerns have emerged that require additional analysis. This process has allowed us to ensure thorough review while maintaining constructive working relationships with applicants. Under the proposed rule, we would be forced to choose between issuing certifications with conditions based on incomplete information (which may be challenged as arbitrary and capricious) or denying certification outright (which may be unnecessarily restrictive if the project could be modified to address concerns). 5 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe The Band urges EPA to retain the current provisions for automatic extensions and to allow certifying authorities and applicants to agree on withdrawal and resubmittal when appropriate. These provisions provide necessary flexibility to accommodate legitimate circumstances while still maintaining the one-year statutory maximum. If EPA is concerned about potential abuse of these provisions, it should provide guidance on appropriate use rather than eliminating them entirely. III. The Proposed Limitation of Certification Scope to “Discharges” Rather Than “Activity as a Whole” Contradicts Supreme Court Precedent, Ignores Scientific Reality, and Would Severely Undermine Water Quality Protection The proposed rule would fundamentally alter the scope of Section 401 certification by limiting review to whether “the discharge” from a proposed project will comply with water quality requirements, rather than whether “the activity as a whole” will comply as provided in the current 2023 Rule. This change represents a dramatic narrowing of certifying authority to review project impacts and is inconsistent with the Clean Water Act’s text, Supreme Court precedent, and scientific understanding of how activities affect water quality. Section 401 requires certification that “any discharge” from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable provisions of the Clean Water Act and with “any other appropriate requirement of State law.” The statute uses the term “discharge” to trigger the certification requirement, but directs certifying authorities to assess whether the discharge will comply with water quality requirements—an assessment that necessarily requires consideration of the activity causing the discharge, the characteristics of that activity, and how the activity affects water quality through the discharge and other mechanisms. In PUD No. 1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology (1994), the Supreme Court explicitly held that states may consider the broader impacts of the activity when evaluating compliance with water quality standards. The Court stated that “the language of § 401(d) cannot reasonably be read to limit a State’s authority to impose conditions on any discharge that may result from the activity which requires a federal license or permit’ and that “[a]lthough § 401(d) authorizes the State to place restrictions on the activity as a whole, that authority is not unbounded.” The Court recognized that minimum flow requirements necessary to maintain water quality, even though not directly regulating the discharge itself, were permissible certification conditions because they were necessary to ensure compliance with water quality standards. EPA’s proposed interpretation that certifying authorities may only review “the discharge” and may not consider the activity-as-a-whole conflicts with this holding. EPA attempts to 6 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe distinguish PUD No. 1 by arguing that the case was decided under Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc. (1984) (“Chevron deference”) and that Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024) requires a new interpretation focused solely on the statutory text. However, PUD No. 1 was not based solely on Chevron deference to EPA’s interpretation; the Court independently analyzed the statutory language and concluded that certification authority extends to conditions affecting the activity as a whole when necessary to ensure water quality compliance. Nothing in Loper Bright overrules this statutory interpretation. Moreover, the proposed scope limitation is scientifically indefensible and would make it impossible for certifying authorities to meaningfully assess water quality compliance. Water quality is affected by numerous aspects of project construction and operation beyond the point source discharge itself. Hydropower dams affect water temperature, dissolved oxygen, sediment transport, fish passage, and flow regimes through their operation regardless of whether they have a discrete point source discharge. Pipeline construction affects sedimentation, habitat destruction, riparian vegetation, and stream channel stability. Mining operations affect groundwater hydrology, stream chemistry, and aquatic habitat through activities that extend far beyond the immediate discharge point. Under the proposed rule, certifying authorities would be prohibited from imposing conditions to address these impacts, even though they directly affect water quality and compliance with water quality standards. The proposed scope limitation would be particularly harmful to Tribal nations whose water quality standards include narrative criteria for aquatic life protection, threatened and endangered species, cultural resources, and traditional uses. For example, the Band’s water quality standards include criteria for the protection of wild rice (manoomin), which requires specific flow regimes, water levels, and habitat conditions that cannot be protected by regulating only the point source discharge. Similarly, water quality standards for cold water fisheries may require maintaining specific temperature regimes, which in turn may require operational conditions for hydropower dams, flow releases, or other project features beyond the discharge itself. The Band urges EPA to retain the current scope provision in the 2023 Rule allowing certifying authorities to review the activity as a whole when assessing whether discharges will comply with water quality requirements. This interpretation is consistent with PUD No. 1, respects the scientific reality of how activities affect water quality, and provides certifying authorities with the tools necessary to fulfill their statutory obligation to ensure water quality compliance. If EPA believes that some limitation on scope is necessary, it should provide clear guidance on the types of conditions that are appropriate rather than imposing a categorical restriction that makes meaningful water quality protection impossible. 7 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe IV. The Proposed Requirements for Contents of Certification Decisions Impose Unnecessary Federal Mandates While Failing to Address Legitimate Concerns About Transparency The proposed rule would require all certification decisions to include specific enumerated contents, including a statement that the discharge will or will not comply with water quality requirements, identification of applicable water quality requirements, a brief statement of the basis for the determination, and other specified information. While transparency in certification decisions is important, the proposed requirements impose rigid federal mandates that fail to respect the diversity of state and Tribal certification processes and that may not enhance meaningful understanding of certification decisions. The current 2023 Rule allows certifying authorities to structure their certification decisions according to their own procedural requirements and legal frameworks, while ensuring that decisions clearly state whether certification is granted, granted with conditions, denied, or waived, and that any conditions are identified. This flexible approach accommodates different state and Tribal administrative law systems, different formats for documenting governmental decisions, and different approaches to public participation and transparency. The proposed requirements would create particular challenges for Tribal governments, which may have certification processes integrated with other Tribal governmental procedures, may document decisions in formats consistent with Tribal traditions and practices, and may need to address issues beyond the narrow scope EPA proposes. For example, Tribal certification decisions may need to address treaty rights implications, cultural resource concerns, traditional ecological knowledge, and government-to-government consultation requirements that do not fit neatly into EPA’s prescribed format. Furthermore, the requirement for a “brief statement of the basis for the determination” may be inconsistent with state and Tribal administrative law requirements for detailed findings of fact and conclusions of law, comprehensive environmental analyses, or specific responses to public comments. Different jurisdictions have different standards for what constitutes an adequate administrative record, and federal imposition of a single format could create conflicts with state and Tribal legal requirements. The Band urges EPA to provide guidance on best practices for certification decision content and transparency while allowing certifying authorities to structure their decisions according to their own legal frameworks. If EPA believes certain minimum information must be included, it should focus on ensuring that decisions clearly state the outcome, identify any conditions, and provide 8 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe adequate explanation of the basis for the decision, rather than prescribing detailed formatting requirements. V. The Proposed Restrictions on Certification Modifications Would Eliminate Necessary Flexibility to Address Changed Circumstances and Would Lock In Outdated Conditions The proposed rule would allow modifications to certification decisions only when the Federal licensing or permitting agency and the applicant agree to both the modification and its specific content. This requirement would effectively give applicants veto power over any modifications, even when changed circumstances, new scientific information, or updated water quality standards demonstrate that modifications are necessary to ensure continued water quality compliance. Section 401(a)(1) requires that any conditions in a certification “shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit.” This language establishes that certification conditions have legal force and must be incorporated into Federal licenses and permits. Nothing in the statute suggests that certifying authorities may not modify conditions when necessary to ensure ongoing water quality compliance. To the contrary, the Act’s objective to “restore and maintain” water quality suggests that certifying authorities must have authority to adapt requirements as understanding evolves and as circumstances change. The proposed modification restrictions would be particularly problematic for long-term licenses and permits such as FERC hydropower licenses, which may last 30 to 50 years. During such extended periods, water quality standards may be updated, new protected species may be listed, climate change may alter flow regimes and temperature patterns, and scientific understanding may advance. Under the proposed rule, certifying authorities would be unable to modify certification conditions to address these changed circumstances without the consent of the applicant and Federal agency, even when existing conditions are demonstrably inadequate to protect water quality. This restriction would be especially harmful to Tribal nations whose treaty resources are affected by long-term Federal licenses and permits. For example, if a hydro-electric power dam licensed decades ago is causing unanticipated impacts on fish passage, water temperature, or flow regimes that affect the Band’s treaty fishing rights, we should be able to request modification of certification conditions to address these impacts without requiring the project proponent to consent. The proposed rule would prevent such modifications and would effectively allow private parties to override Tribal treaty rights and water quality standards. 9 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe The Band urges EPA to remove the requirement for applicant and Federal agency consent to modifications and to instead provide a framework that allows certifying authorities to initiate modifications when necessary to ensure water quality compliance, while providing applicants with appropriate notice and opportunities to respond. Modifications should be subject to the same standards of review as initial certification decisions, including requirements to demonstrate that conditions are necessary to ensure compliance with water quality requirements, but should not require applicant consent. VI. The Proposed Revisions to Section 401(a)(2) Neighboring Jurisdiction Procedures Would Undermine Interstate and Inter-Tribal Coordination and Create Unrealistic Timelines The proposed rule would establish specific timelines and requirements for Section 401(a)(2) notifications to neighboring jurisdictions and would limit the circumstances under which EPA determines that a discharge “may affect” another jurisdiction’s water quality. These changes would reduce opportunities for interstate and inter-Tribal coordination, would create artificial barriers to downstream jurisdictions protecting their water quality, and would impose unrealistic timelines that fail to account for the complexity of evaluating upstream impacts. Section 401(a)(2) recognizes that water pollution does not respect jurisdictional boundaries and that downstream states and Tribes have legitimate interests in ensuring that upstream discharges do not impair their water quality. The provision requires EPA to notify any other state whose water quality may be affected by a discharge and provides those states with opportunities to object to Federal licenses or permits and to request hearings. This process is essential to protecting downstream water quality and to resolving interstate and inter-Tribal water quality disputes. The proposed rule would narrow the circumstances under which EPA determines that a discharge “may affect” downstream water quality by focusing primarily on whether the discharge would cause or contribute to violations of downstream water quality standards. This standard is too restrictive and fails to account for situations where upstream discharges may not cause immediate standard violations but may contribute to cumulative degradation, may affect designated uses without violating numeric criteria, or may raise concerns about antidegradation or downstream impacts that should be addressed through coordination between jurisdictions. Moreover, the proposed timelines for downstream jurisdiction review—60 days for objections and 90 days for Federal agency consideration—are inadequate for meaningful evaluation of complex upstream impacts. Downstream jurisdictions may need to conduct modeling, collect water quality data, assess potential impacts on designated uses and threatened 10 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe species, consult with affected communities, and coordinate with other agencies. The proposed rigid timelines would force premature objections based on incomplete information or would result in the loss of downstream jurisdiction opportunities to protect their waters. For the Band, Section 401(a)(2) is particularly important because many of our treaty- protected waters cross state boundaries or are downstream from activities in other jurisdictions. We have treaty fishing rights in boundary waters and in rivers that flow through or form the boundaries of our ceded territories. Upstream discharges in Wisconsin or other areas can affect water quality in our treaty waters, and we depend on Section 401(a)(2) to ensure that we have opportunities to protect those waters from upstream impacts. The Band urges EPA to retain a broad interpretation of “may affect” that allows EPA to make notifications whenever there is a reasonable possibility that a discharge could impact downstream water quality, not just when violations are likely. EPA should also provide more realistic timelines that accommodate the need for comprehensive evaluation of upstream impacts while still ensuring efficient processing of Federal licenses and permits. VII. The Proposed Elimination of Section 401 Treatment as a State (TAS) Provisions Would Create Barriers to Tribal Self-Determination and Would Undermine Tribal Water Quality Protection The proposed rule would eliminate the provisions in the current regulations establishing procedures for Tribes to obtain treatment as a state (TAS) for purposes of Section 401 certification. EPA proposes instead to rely on the existing TAS procedures for Section 303(c) water quality standards, arguing that Tribes approved for 303(c) TAS automatically have authority to conduct Section 401 certifications. While this interpretation may be technically correct, the elimination of specific Section 401 TAS provisions would create confusion, reduce transparency, and fail to address unique issues related to Section 401 implementation. Section 518 of the Clean Water Act authorizes EPA to treat eligible Tribes in a similar manner as states for purposes of implementing various Clean Water Act programs, including water quality standards and water quality certification. The current regulations include specific provisions addressing TAS for Section 401, including criteria that Tribes must meet and procedures that EPA follows in reviewing TAS applications. These provisions provide clarity about the requirements and process, assist Tribes in developing applications, and ensure consistent implementation across EPA regions. The proposed elimination of these provisions would create several problems. First, it would eliminate guidance that many Tribes have relied upon in developing their water quality 11 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe programs and in planning for eventual assumption of Section 401 authority. Tribes that are developing water quality standards programs often do so with the expectation that they will eventually seek Section 401 certification authority, and the specific regulatory provisions help them understand what will be required. Second, the proposed approach fails to address the unique aspects of Section 401 implementation that may not be fully covered by Section 303(c) TAS criteria. For example, Section 401 requires certifying authorities to coordinate with Federal licensing and permitting agencies, to evaluate compliance with both Federal effluent limitations and state/Tribal water quality requirements, and to make determinations within statutory timeframes. Tribes seeking Section 401 authority need to demonstrate capacity in these specific areas, and the elimination of Section 401 TAS provisions removes the framework for assessing this capacity. Third, eliminating Section 401 TAS provisions may create confusion about whether Tribes with existing 303(c) TAS have Section 401 authority, what the scope of that authority is, and what procedures apply. While EPA states that the regulatory text is unnecessary because Tribes with 303(c) TAS automatically have Section 401 authority, the absence of clear regulatory provisions may lead to disputes about Tribal authority and may discourage Tribes from exercising their certification authority. The Band has not yet sought TAS for Section 401, but we are developing our water quality program with the long-term goal of assuming greater authority over activities affecting our waters. The proposed elimination of Section 401 TAS provisions would create uncertainty about the path forward and would reduce the guidance available to assist us in program development. The Band urges EPA to retain specific provisions for Section 401 TAS that address the unique requirements of the certification program. At a minimum, EPA should issue comprehensive guidance on Section 401 TAS that addresses all of the topics currently covered in the regulations and that provides Tribes with clear information about eligibility criteria, application procedures, and implementation requirements. CONCLUSION The proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Regulations represent a fundamental departure from the balanced framework established by the 2023 Rule and would significantly undermine state and Tribal authority to protect water quality. 12 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe While EPA frames these changes as necessary to increase efficiency and reduce regulatory burden, the proposed rule would sacrifice meaningful water quality protection, would impose rigid federal mandates that fail to respect regional variation and cooperative federalism, and would create barriers to Tribal self-determination and treaty rights protection. For the Band, the consequences of this rule are particularly severe. Our treaty rights to hunt, fish, and gather depend directly on the health and integrity of waters within our ceded territories and reservation. These waters support fish and wildlife populations, sustain manoomin habitat, maintain cultural landscapes, and provide the foundation for our identity as Anishinaabe people. Section 401 serves as a critical tool enabling us to ensure that federally-permitted projects do not degrade these waters or impair our treaty rights. The proposed rule would restrict our ability to obtain necessary information about projects affecting our waters, would force us to make premature decisions with incomplete information, would prevent us from considering the full range of impacts on water quality, would lock us into outdated conditions even when changed circumstances require adjustment, and would eliminate provisions supporting Tribal capacity development and self-determination. These restrictions would make it impossible for us to fulfill our governmental responsibility to protect water quality and would directly impair our ability to protect treaty resources. Moreover, the proposed rule fails to adequately account for Tribal impacts or to fulfill the Federal Government’s trust responsibilities to Tribal nations. The regulatory impact analysis provides no detailed assessment of how the rule would affect Tribal governments, treaty resources, or cultural practices. EPA has not demonstrated that the proposed changes are necessary, that they would actually improve the efficiency of the certification process, or that the benefits of reduced regulatory burden would outweigh the costs of diminished water quality protection. The Band strongly urges EPA to withdraw this proposed rule in its entirety and to retain the 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule. The 2023 Rule provides a balanced framework that respects state and Tribal authority, provides clear procedures, and maintains strong water quality protection. It was developed through extensive stakeholder engagement, comprehensive Tribal consultation, and careful consideration of implementation challenges. Further revisions are unnecessary and would create additional uncertainty, litigation, and harm to water quality. If EPA nevertheless proceeds with revising the 2023 Rule, we strongly urge adoption of the following recommendations: 13 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929— Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe 1. Retain the authority of certifying authorities to define additional contents for certification requests tailored to their water quality standards and local conditions, while providing guidance to ensure transparency and predictability. 2. Maintain automatic extensions for public notice procedures and force majeure events, and allow certifying authorities and applicants to agree on withdrawal and resubmittal when appropriate. 3. Preserve the scope provision in the 2023 Rule allowing certifying authorities to review the activity as a whole when assessing water quality compliance, consistent with Supreme Court precedent in PUD No. 1 and scientific understanding of how activities affect water quality. 4. Allow certifying authorities to structure certification decisions according to their own legal frameworks rather than imposing rigid federal formatting requirements. 5. Remove the requirement for applicant and Federal agency consent to modifications and allow certifying authorities to modify conditions when necessary to ensure water quality compliance. 6. Retain a broad interpretation of “may affect” for Section 401(a)(2) notifications and provide realistic timelines for downstream jurisdiction review. 7. Maintain specific provisions for Section 401 TAS that address the unique requirements of the certification program and support Tribal capacity development. The United States has trust responsibilities to protect Tribal treaty rights and natural resources. These responsibilities cannot be fulfilled by systematically reducing Tribal authority to protect water quality and by imposing federal mandates that fail to account for Tribal governmental structures, cultural values, and implementation capacity. We call on EPA to honor these responsibilities by withdrawing this proposed rule and maintaining the balanced framework of the 2023 Rule. Respectfully submitted, Kelly Applegate (Feb 10, 2026 11:43:06 CST) Kelly Applegate Commissioner of Natural Resources Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe cc: Susan Klapel, Executive Director of Natural Resources, Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe Perry Bunting, Director of Environmental Programs, Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe 14 20260213 EPA CWA401 Certification Revisions Final Audit Report 2026-02-10 Created: 2026-02-10 By: Charlie Lippert (Charlie.Lippert@millelacsband.com) Status: Signed Transaction ID: CBJCHBCAABAA9lPzOyiT9bartn4mVyNiMsvvX8I5eibV "20260213 EPA CWA401 Certification Revisions" History Document created by Charlie Lippert (Charlie.Lippert@millelacsband.com) 2026-02-10 - 1:42:24 PM GMT Document emailed to kelly.applegate@millelacsband.com for signature 2026-02-10 - 1:43:26 PM GMT Email viewed by kelly.applegate@millelacsband.com 2026-02-10 - 5:42:16 PM GMT Signer kelly.applegate@millelacsband.com entered name at signing as Kelly Applegate 2026-02-10 - 5:43:04 PM GMT Document e-signed by Kelly Applegate (kelly.applegate@millelacsband.com) Signature Date: 2026-02-10 - 5:43:06 PM GMT - Time Source: server Agreement completed. 2026-02-10 - 5:43:06 PM GMT
